items.q: What are James's four characteristics of mystical experience?
items.correct: Ineffability (beyond words), noetic quality (gives knowledge), transience (temporary), and passivity (feeling acted upon by something greater).
items.misconception: Religious experiences are just hallucinations caused by stress.
items.random_wrong: Religious experiences only happen during dreams.
items.why: James was a psychologist describing common features, not assessing truth. Students collapse the question of describing experience into the question of whether God caused it — distinct issues.

items.q: What is distinctive about the ontological argument?
items.correct: It tries to prove God's existence purely from the concept of God — a priori reasoning needing no empirical evidence about the world.
items.misconception: The ontological argument uses scientific evidence to prove God exists.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is the same as the cosmological argument.
items.why: Anselm's argument is famously a priori — pure reasoning from definition. Students try to fit it into evidence-based frameworks, missing what makes it uniquely controversial in philosophy.

items.q: What is substance dualism about mind and body?
items.correct: The view (associated with Descartes) that mind and body are two distinct kinds of substance — a non-physical mind interacting with a physical body.
items.misconception: Substance dualism is the belief that humans have two personalities.
items.random_wrong: Substance dualism means men and women are different.
items.why: Students misread 'dualism' as referring to two of anything. It specifically refers to two kinds of substance (mental and physical) — a metaphysical position with deep philosophical implications.

items.q: Does the via negativa mean we can say nothing about God at all?
items.correct: No — it holds we speak truly of God by negation (God is not finite, not material), preserving transcendence while still conveying understanding; it is a way of speaking, not silence or atheism.
items.misconception: The via negativa teaches that God does not exist, since nothing can be said about him.
items.random_wrong: The via negativa says we should only describe God using scientific language.
items.why: Apophatic theologians like Pseudo-Dionysius were devout believers: negation protects God from being reduced to a creature. Students mistake disciplined negation for scepticism.

items.q: When Tillich calls religious language 'symbolic', does he mean it is untrue?
items.correct: No — for Tillich symbols are powerful precisely because they participate in the reality they point to and open up levels of reality otherwise closed; 'symbolic' is not 'merely metaphorical' or false.
items.misconception: Tillich's claim that God-talk is symbolic means he thought religious statements are false.
items.random_wrong: Tillich believed religious language must be taken literally word for word.
items.why: Tillich distinguishes symbols from arbitrary signs: the flag participates in the nation's power. Reading 'symbolic' as 'untrue' inverts his intention to defend religious language's depth.

items.q: Whose 1936 book applied verification to religious language?
items.why: Correct answer: A.J. Ayer. “Antony Flew” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Hare's term for an unfalsifiable yet life-shaping attitude is what?
items.why: Correct answer: Blik. “Parable” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Language, Truth and Logic published?
items.why: The real figure is 1936. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
